content:
Stochastic approximation ideas date to **Robbins and Monro (1951)**, who proved that noisy unbiased estimates of a gradient can drive a sequence to a root or minimum if step sizes satisfy decay conditions—a foundation for modern SGD theory.

**Rosenblatt's perceptron (1958)** and early neural-network work updated weights from one example at a time, an early form of pure SGD.

In the 1980s–90s, **backpropagation** made efficient gradient computation for multi-layer networks practical; **LeCun**, **Hinton**, and others trained models with small batches on available hardware.

The deep learning resurgence (2012 onward) standardized **mini-batch SGD on GPUs**, where batching aligns with parallel matrix multiply. Frameworks like **Theano**, **TensorFlow**, and **PyTorch** baked in data loaders and SGD optimizers as the default training loop.

**Bottou** and colleagues clarified terminology and practice—distinguishing online, stochastic, and mini-batch methods—and modern work on **generalization vs batch size** and **distributed SGD** continues to refine how practitioners choose batch size and learning rate.

timeline.label: Robbins–Monro (1951)
timeline.description: Stochastic approximation theory: noisy gradient steps can converge with properly decreasing step sizes.

timeline.label: Backprop + batched neural nets (1980s–90s)
timeline.description: Efficient gradients and mini-batch updates make multi-layer network training feasible.

timeline.label: GPU mini-batch era (2010s–present)
timeline.description: Deep learning frameworks treat mini-batch SGD as the default loop; data-parallel training scales batch size across devices.
